step.title: Step 12
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Move light green left to edge of gray box')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 13, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board is split vertically by a white column divider at col 32-33. Left side has a dark gray background with several colored objects. Right side has a black background with objects. Need to understand the object layout and goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 12 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! I notice that the bottom gray box (id=30) now has NO children! The light green square (id=31) is now a separate object, not a child of the gray box. This is different from the initial state where the light green was a child of the gray box.
Wait, looking more carefully:
- id=30 color=g px=32 [38:43,8:13] children=[]
- id=31 color=N px=4 [38:39,10:11] children=[]
The light green is no longer a child of the gray box! This happened after I moved it with UP. Initially, the light green was inside the gray box as a child. After moving it, it's no longer a child.
Also, the yellow square is still at [20:21, 24:25], inside the top gray box [18:23, 22:27]. Let me check if the yellow is still a child of the top gray box.
Looking at the output:
- id=6 color=g px=32 [18:23,22:27] children=[9]
- id=9 is the yellow square
So the yellow is still a child of the top gray box. The light green is no longer a child of the bottom gray box because I moved it.
Wait, but the light green is still visually inside the gray box. The gray box is at [38:43, 8:13], and the light green is at [38:39, 10:11]. So it's still within the bounds of the gray box. The "children" relationship might have changed because the light green moved to the edge of the gray box, so it's no longer "fully enclosed."
Actually, looking at the boundary, the gray box goes from row 38 to 43, and the light green is at rows 38-39. Since the light green touches the top edge of the gray box, it's no longer "fully enclosed" (children are objects fully enclosed by the parent).
OK, so the light green can move within the gray box. Let me think about the goal again. Maybe the goal is to move the colored squares to specific positions within their gray boxes, matching some pattern.
Let me look at the right-side shapes more carefully. The red shape is at [17:21, 42:54] and the blue shape is at [34:38, 42:54]. These are irregular shapes.
Wait, maybe the goal is to position the colored squares in the gray boxes to match the "silhouettes" of the right-side shapes? The gray boxes are 6x6, and the right shapes are larger. But maybe the colored squares trace a path within the gray boxes?
Actually, let me think about this differently. The game might be a "painting" or "filling" puzzle. The right-side shapes are the target patterns, and the left-side blocks are the "canvas." The colored squares in the gray boxes are the "brushes" that paint the canvas.
But I've been clicking on the right-side shapes, and they toggle the left-side blocks. Maybe the goal is to:
1. Click on the right-side shapes to transform the left blocks
2. Move the colored squares to specific positions
3. Achieve a configuration where the left blocks match the right shapes
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the initial state more carefully and understand what the "default" configuration is, then figure out what the target configuration should be.
Actually, I just realized something. The initial state had the light green square centered in the bottom gray box, and the yellow square centered in the top gray box. Maybe the goal is to move them to specific positions. Let me try moving the light green to different positions and see if the level completes.
But first, let me reset the light green to its original position and think about the game more carefully. I've used 12 actions so far, and the target is about 59. So I have room to explore.
Let me try moving the light green to the left edge of the gray box and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try moving the light green square to the left edge
# Currently at [38:39, 10:11], gray box is [38:43, 8:13]
# Left edge would be col 9 (1 cell from left wall at col 8)

predict(expect_change=True, note='Move light green left to edge of gray box')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== After LEFT ===")
for r in range(37, 44):
    print(f"  {r}: {ascii_lines[r][7:14]}")

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"  Light green: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
=== After LEFT ===
  37: cbcbccc
  38: cNNgggg
  39: cNNgggg
  40: cgggggg
  41: cgggggg
  42: cgggggg
  43: cgggggg
  Light green: [38:39,8:9]
